We definitely don't want to re-run `__init__` when the host changes. Beyond being bad practice this is going to have a lot of unintended side-effects, such as dropping all the old kwargs passed in.

What we'll want to do is pull everything from init that needs to be re-run when the host changes into a separate method, and call that method in init, and here. ([Probably everything from here down.](https://github.com/balloob/pychromecast/blob/master/pychromecast/__init__.py#L174))

This was a tricky design question, actually.  I wasn't sure whether to have `more_screens = galsim.PhaseScreens(screens)` make a deep copy or reference the items in the original screens.  It turns out that regular python lists make references.  For example:

```
# List of immutables
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = list(a)

a[0] = 4  # Manipulate original, doesn't affect copy
print a  # [4, 2, 3]
print b  # [1, 2, 3]

# List of mutables
a = [[1], [2], [3]]
b = list(a)
a[0] = [4]  # Manipulate original list by replacing item with new object, doesn't affect copy.
print a  # [[4], [2], [3]]
print b  # [[1], [2], [3]]

# List of mutables
a = [[1], [2], [3]]
b = list(a)
a[0][0] = 4  # Mutate item in original list in place, *does* affect copy.
print a  # [[4], [2], [3]]
print b  # [[4], [2], [3]]
```

Since reset() mutates the screen in place, it affects both `screens` and `more_screens`.  I could have programmed this the other way, by explicitly making a deep copy, but I thought it best to match regular python lists as much as possible.

- pour chaque dispositif de l'impôt (réductions, crédits, etc) j'utilise les cases en les nommant par leur numéro

A configuration parameter declared as `alt_features` will pick up any environment variable named `CONDA_ALT_FEATURES`.  So it's already taken care of.  Similarly, naming the argparse flag `--alt-features`, which turns into `args.alt_features`, will also be available as `context.alt_features`.  That is, all available configuration sources are merged into `context`, in the order `SEARCH_PATH`, environment variables, argparse args.

I don't know what a better name is yet either.  But, if it's undocumented right now, then I don't care quite as much.

`sign_request` now looks like this:

```
def sign_request(self, prepared_request):
    headers = self.request_signer.get_signature_headers(
        url=prepared_request.path_url, body=prepared_request.body
    )
    prepared_request.headers.update(headers)
    return prepared_request
```
so `api_key` is no longer needed by `sign_request`, because `sign_request` delegates the task to of signing to `self.request_signer`, which was defined in `__init__` like so:
```
 self.request_signer = RequestSigner(secret=api_key)
```
